title: Black Sesame Cookies
tagline: Chewy sugar cookies with the flavor of the year.
description: Crackly-edged, chewy-centered sugar cookies loaded with toasted black sesame - nutty, faintly bitter, halfway between tahini and dark chocolate. Black sesame is having its all-time-high moment; this is the easiest way in.
ingredients: 100g (1/2 cup) granulated sugar, plus more for rolling
50g (3 Tbsp) black sesame paste (or make your own - see notes)
2 Tbsp black sesame seeds, for rolling
steps: Preheat oven to 350°F (175°C). Line two sheet pans with parchment.
Beat butter and both sugars until light and fluffy, 2-3 minutes.
Beat in the egg, then the black sesame paste and vanilla, until evenly slate-gray.
Whisk flour, baking soda, and salt; mix into the wet ingredients just until no dry streaks remain.
Roll 2-Tbsp balls, then roll each in a mix of granulated sugar and black sesame seeds.
Bake 11-12 minutes until the edges are set and the centers still look slightly underdone. They crackle as they cool.
Cool on the pan 5 minutes - they finish setting there - then move to a rack.
notes: DIY paste: toast 70g black sesame seeds in a dry skillet 2-3 minutes until fragrant, then blend with 1 Tbsp neutral oil and 1 Tbsp honey until smooth-ish.
Black sesame paste (often labeled 'kuro neri goma') is at Japanese and Korean groceries - unsweetened is what you want here.
Underbaking is the chew. If they look done in the oven, they're overdone on the counter.
testedNote: Weighed, not scooped - this dough punishes extra flour with cakey cookies.

verdict.note: Tastes sophisticated, bakes like a snickerdoodle - the highest flavor-to-effort ratio on this site.

troubleshooting: Cakey, puffy cookies → too much flour or overmixed; weigh the flour, stop at no-streaks.
Spread into puddles → butter too soft or pans hot; chill dough 15 minutes between batches.
Bitter edge → seeds over-toasted; black sesame goes from fragrant to acrid fast.
Dry, crumbly dough → paste was stiff old-jar sludge; stir the jar's oil back in first.
